context: You can read of that in 1 Kings 19, verses 15 through 17.
point_summary: God's reassurance to the despairing Elijah — "I have a plan for you" — is cited as a biblical illustration that God holds purposeful plans for individuals even when they are ready to give up.

context: when God appeared to Solomon, and He said to Solomon, ask what I shall give thee
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom when God invited him to ask for anything illustrates the kind of desire for divine knowledge and good judgment that the sermon holds up as a model for believers.

context: what did Solomon request? Wisdom. Wisdom. God, give me wisdom to lead this great people
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom to lead God's people—rather than wealth or long life—illustrates the kind of God-honoring desire for knowledge and judgment that the sermon holds up as a model.

context: God gave Solomon wisdom, in fact, he's the wisest man according to the Scriptures
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In 1 Kings chapter number 9, we'll finish up with this particular passage of Scripture dealing with this
point_summary: The fulfillment of Noah's curse on Canaan—that he would be a servant of servants—is traced all the way to Solomon's reign, demonstrating the prophecy's long historical reach across centuries.

context: In 1 Kings chapter number 9, in verses 20 and 21.
point_summary: Even at the height of Solomon's reign—centuries after Genesis 9—the Canaanite descendants remained under a levy of bond service, demonstrating the long-range fulfillment of Noah's prophecy.

context: In 1 Kings chapter number 9, in verses 20 and 21.
point_summary: Even as late as Solomon's reign, the Canaanite descendants remained under forced tribute, demonstrating the long-range fulfillment of Noah's prophecy across centuries of biblical history.

context: it tells us in the beginning of the passage in verse number 1, and it came to pass when Solomon had finished the building of the house of the Lord, and the king's house, and all Solomon's desire, which he was pleased to do.
point_summary: 1 Kings 9:1 anchors the timeline at Solomon's completed temple, marking how far Noah's prophecy of Canaanite servitude had already stretched across redemptive history.

context: God got my attention in 1 Kings 19 this past week on a question that he asked Elijah...
point_summary: God's reminder to Elijah that 7,000 others remained faithful counters the isolating lie that one is the only follower of Christ, underscoring the need to find and run with fellow believers.

context: I want you to think about the raven in 1 Kings chapter number 17. Elijah was sent to a brook. And God says this to Elijah, I have commanded there a raven to feed thee.
point_summary: The obedience of the raven commanded to feed Elijah illustrates that God's creation obeys its Creator, setting a standard that redeemed people ought to follow.

context: Jezebel said in first Kings with regard to Elijah in first Kings chapter number 18, right actually it's in chapter number 19 that she says After she hears word about Elisha slaying the prophets of Baal. She says this Let the gobs do to me and more also if I make not thy life as the life of one of them by tomorrow
point_summary: Jehoram's oath to kill Elisha mirrors almost word-for-word his mother Jezebel's oath against Elijah, revealing that his murderous hostility toward God's prophet is his mother's wicked spirit reproduced in her son.

context: That was in first Kings 19 and verse number 2 when Jezebel is speaking about killing the man of God Elijah
point_summary: Jezebel's oath to kill Elijah (1 Kings 19:2) is set in deliberate parallel with Jehoram's oath to kill Elisha, showing a son repeating his mother's murderous pattern against God's prophet.

context: if you go back to 1 Kings, maybe jot it down in your notes, in 1 Kings chapter number 12, in verses 25 through 33, you will find described for you in that passage of scripture, the sins of Jeroboam.
point_summary: First Kings 12:25–33 is cited as the foundational record of Jeroboam's idolatrous innovations—false worship, counterfeit feast days—instituted to prevent Israel from returning to Jerusalem and thereby to Judah's king.

context: you find that detailed in 1 Kings 12, verses 25 through 33
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I want you to also look over, if you will, at 1 Kings 12. In 1 Kings 12, we begin to read of an important time in Israel's history.
point_summary: The tribe of Dan's early introduction of idol worship, traced in Judges 18, is shown to have set a pattern that resurfaces in 1 Kings 12 as a pivotal moment of national idolatry in Israel's history.

context: We find that Israel up to this point in 1 Kings 12, they've had three kings, Saul, David, and Solomon.
point_summary: The divided kingdom described in 1 Kings 12 marks the point at which the northern tribes of Israel entered an unbroken downward spiral of idolatry, contrasted with Judah's periodic revivals under godly kings.

context: after the death of Solomon, at the end of chapter 11, that what happens is there's a divided kingdom.
point_summary: The divided kingdom described at the end of 1 Kings 11 sets the stage for understanding Israel's unbroken downward spiral into idolatry, contrasted with Judah's periodic revivals under good kings.

context: as we read of here in 1 Kings chapter number 12, there was some concern on the part of Jeroboam
point_summary: First Kings 12 marks the division of the united kingdom into northern Israel and southern Judah, with Jeroboam's fear that shared worship in Jerusalem would undermine northern loyalty establishing a pattern of idolatry that defined Israel's kings thereafter.

context: Then Jeroboam built Shechem in Mount Ephraim, and dwelt therein, and went out from Thence, and built Penuel.
point_summary: Jeroboam's fear that Israel's required pilgrimages to Jerusalem would draw the northern tribes back to Rehoboam drove him to establish a rival worship system, giving rise to the recurring biblical indictment "the sins of Jeroboam."

context: And this thing became a sin: for the people went to worship before the one, even unto Dan.
point_summary: Jeroboam's sin of convenience-driven, self-devised worship—marketing idolatry as accessible—stands as a warning against accommodating religious practice to popular preference rather than God's revealed standard.

context: And he made a house of high places, and made priests of the lowest of the people, which were not of the sons of Levi.
point_summary: Jeroboam's appointment of non-Levitical priests from the lowest of the people illustrates how false worship systems discard God's ordained order in favor of convenience and self-devised religion.

context: And Jeroboam ordained a feast in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day of the month, like unto the feast that is in Judah.
point_summary: Jeroboam's feast in the eighth month was a man-made substitute for God's appointed worship, devised from his own heart rather than divine command.

context: And he offered upon the altar, so did he in Bethel, sacrificing unto the calves that he had made.
point_summary: Jeroboam's feast, ordained in a month he devised of his own heart, illustrates how man-made religious convenience replaces God-ordained worship with self-serving substitutes.

context: ...and how they would set up a place and they would make offerings and they would serve these idols?
point_summary: Jeroboam's establishment of golden calves at Bethel and Dan fulfilled the trajectory of Dan's earlier idolatry, illustrating how a tribe's spiritual compromise opens the door to national apostasy.

context: And what happened when he prayed that fire? He said, Lord, I've spoken all these things according to Thy Word.
point_summary: Fire falling from heaven in answer to Elijah's prayer illustrates the power of fervent, faith-filled intercession that the sermon connects to Epaphras's own devoted prayer life.

context: The Bible just declares in verse number one of 1 Kings 17, and Elijah, the Tishbite.
point_summary: Elijah's sudden appearance "out of obscurity" in 1 Kings 17:1, with no prior biblical introduction, underscores that God raises up his servants from unknown places to serve his purposes in critical moments of Israel's history.

context: and the word of the Lord came unto him, Elijah, saying
point_summary: God's word to Elijah in verse 2 initiates the divine provision that follows, showing that obedience to God's command is the pathway through which His care for His servant is expressed.

context: get thee hence and turn thee eastward, and hide thyself by the brook Cherith, that is before Jordan
point_summary: God's command to Elijah to hide himself by the brook Cherith becomes the sermon's governing text, framing the entire message around the theme of divine care for God's servant.

context: And it shall be that thou shalt drink of the brook, and I have commanded the ravens to feed thee there
point_summary: God's sovereign provision for Elijah is shown in His direct command to the ravens, demonstrating that He actively cares for His servants even in seasons of withdrawal and judgment.

context: So he went and did according unto the word of the Lord, for he went and dwelt by the brook Cherith, that is before Jordan
point_summary: Elijah's immediate obedience — "he went and did according unto the word of the Lord" — is presented as the model response to divine instruction, demonstrating that God's care is received through compliance with His command.

context: The ravens brought him bread and flesh in the morning and bread and flesh in the evening, and he drank of the brook
point_summary: God's miraculous provision of bread and flesh twice daily through ravens demonstrates His direct, personal care for Elijah during the season of divinely ordained drought and hiddenness.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number 7, and it came to pass after a while that the brook dried up
point_summary: The drying of the brook Cherith, caused by the very drought Elijah had pronounced, frames God's providential care as operating even through circumstances that appear to remove His provision.

context: Ahab made a grove, and Ahab did more to provoke the Lord God of Israel to anger than all the kings of Israel that were before him.
point_summary: Ahab's surpassing wickedness among Israel's kings establishes the gravity of the divine judgment Elijah was sent to announce before him.

context: The Bible says, and it shall be that thou shalt drink of the brook. And then notice this promise. And I have commanded the ravens to feed thee there.
point_summary: God's clear guidance to Elijah at Cherith came paired with a specific promise of provision, as the divine command to the ravens demonstrates that God's direction to his servants never comes without accompanying care.

context: The obedience of Elijah is noted in verse number five of our text of scripture, where the Bible simply says this, so he, speaking of Elijah, went and did according unto the word of the Lord.
point_summary: Elijah's simple obedience—"he went and did according unto the word of the Lord"—is presented as the necessary condition for receiving God's promised sustenance, since the ravens were commanded to feed him *there*, at the specific place God designated.

context: the Bible says that he drank of the brook in verse number six
point_summary: God's sovereignty over creation is on display as ravens — natural scavengers — obeyed His command and delivered bread and flesh to Elijah twice daily, evidencing His personal, consistent provision for His servant.

context: Elijah and the ravens
point_summary: Jesus's reference to ravens feeding in Luke 12 draws a direct line back to Elijah's ravens in 1 Kings 17, reinforcing that the same God who fed the prophet supernaturally cares far more for his disciples.

context: Here, God tells us that he feeds the ravens. But in 1 Kings 17, God used the ravens to feed his servant.
point_summary: God's care for his servant Elijah is demonstrated in the remarkable reversal at Cherith, where the ravens he ordinarily feeds became the instruments by which he fed his prophet.

context: I read about Elijah, 1 Kings chapter 18, Lord God of Abraham, he begins to pray, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel, and that I am thy servant, and that I have done all these things at thy word.
point_summary: Elijah's brief, faith-filled prayer on Carmel—answered immediately with fire—is held up as the model of intercession that demonstrates to a watching world who the true God is.

context: ...Elijah, it's as if he just appears out of nowhere in first Kings 17...
point_summary: Elijah's sudden, unannounced appearance in 1 Kings 17 is used as a parallel to illustrate how Melchizedek likewise steps onto the pages of Scripture without introduction or genealogical context.

context: turn to the book of 1 Kings if you will in chapter 3. And we're familiar with the passage of Scripture here in 1 Kings 3 and what's taking place.
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom in 1 Kings 3 is cited to illustrate that God offers His wisdom freely to those who ask, so believers need not rely on their own thinking to resolve conflict.

context: in Gibeon, the Lord appeared to Solomon in a dream by night and God said, ask what I shall give thee.
point_summary: God's open-ended invitation to Solomon — "Ask what I shall give thee" — illustrates that divine wisdom is freely available to those who seek it, contrasting the unlimited offer of the Creator with the hollow offers of earthly rulers like Herod and Ahasuerus.

context: And Solomon said, Thou hast showed unto Thy servant David, my father, great mercy, according as he walked before Thee in truth and in righteousness and in uprightness of heart with Thee.
point_summary: Solomon's response to God's open-ended offer reveals that humility and recognition of God's prior mercy and faithfulness are the posture from which true wisdom is sought.

context: Thou hast given him a son to sit on his throne as it is this day. Do you see in verse 6 the humility of Solomon?
point_summary: Solomon's response to God's open-ended offer reveals humility and gratitude, acknowledging divine mercy and covenant faithfulness to David rather than seizing the moment for personal ambition.

context: And now, O Lord my God, Thou hast made Thy servant king instead of David my father. And I am but a little child.
point_summary: Solomon's self-description as "a little child" who does not know how to lead illustrates the humility that underlies his request for wisdom rather than wealth or power.

context: And Thy servant is in the midst of Thy people which Thou hast chosen a great people that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude.
point_summary: Overwhelmed by the vastness of the people he must govern, Solomon grounds his request for wisdom in humble acknowledgment that the task exceeds his own ability.

context: Give therefore Thy servant an understanding heart to judge Thy people that I may discern between good and bad for who is able to judge this Thy so great a people?
point_summary: Confronted with an open-ended divine offer, Solomon's request in verse 9 for an understanding heart to judge God's people demonstrated the humility and God-ward orientation that pleased the Lord.

context: I love what the Bible tells us in verse 10. And the speech pleased the Lord. That Solomon had asked this thing.
point_summary: God's pleasure with Solomon's request for wisdom — rather than wealth or long life — underscores that humility and a servant's heart in asking are what the Lord finds pleasing.

context: in 1 Kings 3, beginning in verse 16. We don't have time to deal with all of it, but down through verse 28 is the first opportunity for Solomon to display this wisdom that God had supernaturally given him.
point_summary: Verse 16 introduces the test case through which God's supernaturally granted wisdom to Solomon is first publicly demonstrated, as an otherwise unsolvable dispute between two mothers reveals what no merely human judgment could discern.

context: And the Bible says this in verse 22, And the other woman said, nay, but the living is my son and the dead is thy son. And this said, no. But the dead is thy son and the living is my son. Thus they spake before the king.
point_summary: The disputed claims of the two mothers in verse 22 illustrate that apart from God-given wisdom, discerning truth in such a deadlocked human conflict is impossible.

context: Verse 23, Then said the king, the one saith, this is my son that liveth and thy son is the dead. And the other saith, nay, but thy son is the dead and my son is the living.
point_summary: Verse 23 presents the deadlocked dispute between the two mothers as an illustration that without divinely given wisdom, discerning truth in such a case would be humanly impossible.

context: And the king said, divide the living child in two and give half to the one and half to the other.
point_summary: Solomon's impossible judgment — dividing the living child to expose the true mother — is presented as the first public display of the supernatural, God-given wisdom he had asked for.

context: Then spake the woman whose the living child was unto the king in verse 26, for her bowels yearned upon her son. And she said, oh my Lord, give her the living child and in no wise slay it.
point_summary: A mother's instinctive cry to spare her child's life — not the other woman's indifference — revealed the truth that no earthly wisdom could uncover, demonstrating that Solomon's judgment was divinely given.

context: then the king answered and said, give her the living child. living child and in no wise lay it she is the mother thereof
point_summary: Solomon's verdict in verse 27 is presented as the visible proof that divine wisdom—not human reasoning—was operating in him to render true judgment.

context: in verse 28 the Bible says in all Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged and they feared the king for they saw that the wisdom of God was
point_summary: Israel's awe at Solomon's verdict demonstrated that divine wisdom—not human reasoning—was visibly at work in him, making the case that heavenly wisdom is what leaders and believers must seek.

context: one that we mentioned his wisdom that was on display in first Kings chapter number three
point_summary: Solomon's wisdom displayed in 1 Kings 3 is invoked as a parallel to Abram's wise, humble resolution of strife, contrasted with the pride that Proverbs warns brings contention and destruction.

context: Elijah was a man like we're men, human beings. And yet he prayed and it did not rain for the space of three and a half years, right? You read it, 1 Kings 17.
point_summary: Elijah's drought and rain in 1 Kings 17 illustrates James's point that fervent prayer is the exercise of faith, inviting God to act where human ability falls short.

context: if you go to the book of 1 Kings and chapter number 18 and then cross-reference that with James chapter number 5, you will find that Elijah prayed with confidence.
point_summary: Elijah's bold, expectant prayer at Mount Carmel illustrates praying in alignment with God's will—his confidence rooted in the declaration "I have done all of this according to Thy word."

context: still, small voice
point_summary: God's still, small voice requires the posture of attentive, bowed-down listening that Proverbs 22:17 pictures—hearing His will before praying according to it.

context: Elijah calling for a drought
point_summary: Famine in Scripture most often signals divine judgment, as illustrated by connecting Elijah's drought in 1 Kings 17:1 to Israel's wickedness under Ahab and Jezebel.

context: he prayed a prayer and fire came down from heaven
point_summary: Fire coming down from heaven in answer to Elijah's prayer—offered explicitly "according to thy word"—illustrates that confident, effective prayer is grounded in alignment with God's will and word.

context: Elijah and all of the worshippers of Baal and as they're crying out and cutting themselves and all that they're doing to try to get the attention of their false god to send fire.
point_summary: Baal's failure to respond to his worshippers' cries illustrates by contrast that prayer requires confidence in a God who genuinely hears, unlike deaf idols who cannot.

context: First Kings chapter 11. There's nothing really profound about this. It's just really, to me, it's just very, very practical when you stop and think about getting off course.
point_summary: Solomon's life in 1 Kings 11 is presented as a cautionary example of a man who, despite beginning with God-given wisdom, drifted so far off course that his life ended in a way displeasing to the Lord.

context: Remember what Solomon did? If you look back in chapter three, which we will go back there a little bit later on.
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom in 1 Kings 3 is cited as the starting point of a life that nonetheless drifted off course, illustrating that even a God-given gift cannot substitute for sustained obedience.

context: Look at 1 Kings chapter 11, and I'm going to read down through this pretty quickly, so stick with me.
point_summary: Solomon's God-given wisdom and wealth in 1 Kings 11 serve as the backdrop for how a man who began humbly, asking only for wisdom, eventually got off course despite extraordinary divine blessing.

context: If you look at 1 Kings chapter 10, we know the queen of Sheba came to visit him because she said, I've heard about your kingdom.
point_summary: Solomon's display of God-given wealth before the queen of Sheba is read as a moment where pride began to surface, with the broader point that all wealth ultimately comes from God's hand, not human effort.

context: But look in 1 Kings 10, verse 24. After he trots out all this wealth that he has, and she sees all this, and it says, all the earth sought the psalm, and to hear his wisdom, which God had put in his heart.
point_summary: Solomon's unparalleled wealth and wisdom, which drew the whole earth to him, is presented as God's gift rather than any achievement of Solomon's own making.

context: And they brought every man as present, vessels of silver and vessels of gold and garments, and armor and spices and horses and mules, arrayed year by year.
point_summary: All wealth—even that of the heathen—comes from God's hand, not from human effort or self-sufficiency.

context: And Solomon gathered together chariots and horsemen, and he had 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen, whom he bestowed in the cities for chariots with the king of Jerusalem.
point_summary: Solomon's vast accumulation of chariots, horsemen, and silver—catalogued in these verses—is presented as wealth that came entirely from God's hand, not from Solomon's own achievement.

context: And the king made silver to be in Jerusalem, as stones and cedars made he to be as the sycamore trees that are in the veil for abundance.
point_summary: All wealth—even Solomon's extraordinary abundance of silver and cedar—comes from God's hand, not human achievement, making any claim of self-made prosperity an act of arrogance.

context: And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt in linen yarn, and king's merchants received a linen yarn at a price.
point_summary: All wealth—even Solomon's extraordinary accumulation of horses, chariots, silver, and tribute—comes from God's hand, not human achievement, making self-made pride an act of arrogance.

context: And a chariot came up and went out of Egypt, and six hundred shekels of silver, and a horse for a hundred and fifty. And so all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, did they bring him out by their means.
point_summary: All wealth—including Solomon's extraordinary accumulation of chariots, horses, silver, and tribute—comes from God's hand, not human achievement, making self-sufficiency an act of arrogance.

context: But King Solomon loved many strange women, together with the daughter of Pharaoh, women of the Moabites, Ammonites, Idonians, Edomites, Idonians, and Hittites
point_summary: Solomon's marriage to many foreign women from nations God had explicitly forbidden violated divine command and set him on a course of spiritual deviation with guaranteed consequences.

context: of the nations concerning which the Lord sent unto the children of Israel. Ye shall not go into them, neither shall they come in
point_summary: God's explicit prohibition against intermarrying with pagan nations—because such relationships would turn Israel's heart away from Him—is the divine boundary Solomon willfully crossed, with guaranteed consequences.

context: So, he had 700 wives, princesses, and 300 concubines. And his wives, all these women, turned away his heart.
point_summary: Solomon's accumulation of a thousand women, condemned in 1 Kings 11:3, is presented as the compounding moral drift that turned his heart from God and made course correction increasingly difficult.

context: For it came to pass, when Solomon was old, that his wives turned away his heart after other gods. And his heart was not perfect with the Lord, his God, as was the heart of David his father.
point_summary: Solomon's accumulated wives turning his heart away from God illustrates how gradual moral drift compounds until course correction becomes extremely difficult.

context: Now, in 1 Kings chapter 3 the Bible says that Solomon loved the Lord, but he didn't love God as much as his father did.
point_summary: Solomon's love for God in 1 Kings 3:3 is presented as genuine but comparatively shallower than David's, lacking the deep personal intimacy that marked his father's relationship with the Lord.

context: For Solomon went after Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Zidonians, and after Milcom, the abomination of the Ammonites.
point_summary: Solomon's worship of Ashtoreth and Milcom is presented as the tragic endpoint of incremental spiritual drift, with God's own label—"abomination"—signaling that such false worship demanded abominable acts in return.

context: And Solomon did evil in the sight of the LORD, and went not fully after the LORD, as did David his father.
point_summary: Solomon's incremental drift from God culminated in outright evil, confirming that his devotion never matched David's wholehearted love for the Lord.

context: And Solomon did evil in the sight of the LORD, and went not fully after the LORD as did David his father.
point_summary: Solomon's failure is measured not merely by his idolatry but by the contrast with David, whose defining mark was wholehearted devotion and willingness to make course corrections through genuine repentance.

context: And the Lord was angry with Solomon, because his heart was turned from the Lord God of Israel, which had appeared unto him twice.
point_summary: God's anger with Solomon is grounded in the fact that He had appeared to him twice and commanded him directly, making Solomon's disobedience without excuse.

context: Wherefore the Lord said unto Solomon, Forasmuch as this is done of thee, and thou hast not kept my covenant and my statutes which I have commanded thee, I will surely rend the kingdom from thee, and will give it to thy servant.
point_summary: God's judgment on Solomon—tearing the kingdom from him—is presented as the direct consequence of covenant-breaking, with the Hebrew word *rend* (*ohabit*) underscoring the severity of divine discipline for disobedience.

context: Notwithstanding in thy days, I will not take it for David thy father's sake, but I will rend it out of the hand of thy son.
point_summary: God's mercy toward David delayed but did not cancel the judgment on Solomon's kingdom, preserving one tribe for David's sake as the consequence of Solomon's disobedience unfolded across generations.

context: Howbeit I will not rend away all the kingdoms, but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake which I have chosen.
point_summary: God's mercy toward David preserved one tribe for Solomon's son, showing that covenant faithfulness to a prior generation can limit but not eliminate the consequences of a later generation's sin.

context: Now I want to read something to you here very quickly, and then I'm going to look at 1 Kings chapter 3.
point_summary: Solomon's sin was not ignorance but willful rebellion — God had appeared to him twice and commanded him explicitly, leaving him without excuse when his heart turned away.

context: Listen to this, 1 Kings chapter 3 and verse 1, someone asked me not too long ago, well, Brother Meshon where did he get off course?
point_summary: Solomon's first recorded act — marrying Pharaoh's daughter — marks the precise moment he began drifting off course, even before he asked God for wisdom, showing that disobedience can precede and undermine every subsequent blessing.

context: And Solomon made affinity with Pharaoh king of Egypt, and took Pharaoh's daughter, and brought her into the city of David, until he had made an end of building his own house, and the house of the Lord, and the wall of Jerusalem round about.
point_summary: Solomon's first recorded act—marrying Pharaoh's daughter before even asking God for wisdom—is identified as the one-degree deviation that set his entire life's trajectory off course.

context: this evening, 1 Kings chapter 19. I gave you both in Sunday school and also in the morning service, just a word or two about the subject that we'll be dealing with tonight in 1 Kings chapter 19.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Elijah appears on the pages of scripture seemingly out of nowhere in 1 Kings chapter 17.
point_summary: Elijah's sudden appearance before Ahab in 1 Kings 17 introduces him against the backdrop of Israel's deepest moral and spiritual darkness, a dynasty of kings each more wicked than the last.

context: We find that as we read 1 Kings 17 and 18, really a quick read of those two chapters, give us much information about the life of Elijah.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We find that as we read 1 Kings 17 and 18, really a quick read of those two chapters, give us much information about the life of Elijah.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We're gonna really focus on chapter 19 this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And we find that it was through his hands that God would work miracles and we find amazing things that happen. But as you begin reading 1 Kings chapter 19, you find that things change considerably.
point_summary: The great victory at Mount Carmel immediately precedes a sharp reversal, as Ahab's report to Jezebel in verse 1 sets in motion the crisis that will test Elijah's faithfulness.

context: The high point, if you will, in chapter 18 is there at Mount Carmel when fire, God sends fire from heaven to consume the offering and the prophets of Baal are destroyed.
point_summary: 1 Kings 18's Mount Carmel episode—where God sends fire from heaven and the prophets of Baal are destroyed—represents the high point of Elijah's ministry before the dramatic reversal in chapter 19.

context: then Jezebel sent a messenger unto Elijah saying, so let the gods do to me and more also, if I make not thy life as the life of one of them by tomorrow about this time.
point_summary: Jezebel's threat to kill Elijah by the next day establishes the crisis that drives him into flight, revealing the real and powerful opposition that can reduce even a faithful prophet to fear and despair.

context: when he stood there before Ahab in chapter number 17 and verse number one and he delivered that declaration
point_summary: Elijah's 25-word declaration of judgment before Ahab in 1 Kings 17:1 was followed by clear divine direction to Cherith, contrasting sharply with his self-directed flight in chapter 19.

context: after that, the word of the Lord came to Elijah and told Elijah where to go
point_summary: In contrast to chapter 19, 1 Kings 17 shows God giving Elijah clear, specific direction—first to Cherith, then to Zarephath—direction conspicuously absent when Elijah flees Jezebel's threat.

context: God says, go to Cherith, and he goes to Cherith
point_summary: God's clear direction to Elijah at the brook Cherith (17:3) contrasts sharply with chapter 19, where Elijah flees Jezebel's threat without any divine command to depart.

context: the water dries up, why? Because there's a drought
point_summary: The drying of the brook Cherith, though ordained by God who foreknew the drought, illustrates that God was directing Elijah's every step in chapter 17—a divine guidance conspicuously absent when Elijah flees in chapter 19.

context: God speaks to him and says, go to Zarephath
point_summary: God's step-by-step direction to Elijah in chapter 17—first to Cherith, then to Zarephath—contrasts sharply with chapter 19, where Elijah flees without any divine command to do so.

context: you miss in chapter 19
point_summary: Jezebel's threat in verse 1 sets in motion a flight that, unlike Elijah's earlier movements in chapters 17–18, lacks any divine directive to depart.

context: God sent an angel in verse number five, and that angel touched Elijah.
point_summary: Even when Elijah was hidden from Jezebel and sunk in despair, God knew exactly where his servant was and sent an angel to meet his physical need with miraculous provision.

context: Notice verse six, and he looked, and behold, there was a cake baking on the coals, and a cruise of water at his head,
point_summary: Even in Elijah's lowest moment of despair, God supernaturally and miraculously provided exactly what his servant needed — food and water appearing by angelic agency without any human explanation.

context: Notice in verse 14, he said, I have been very jealous for the Lord God of hosts because the children of Israel have forsaken thy covenant, thrown down thine altars and slain thy prophets with the sword and I even I only am left and they seek my life to take it away.
point_summary: Elijah's repeated, unchanged answer to God's repeated question illustrates divine patience: God meets his servant exactly where he is rather than demanding a corrected response before extending grace.

context: and the Lord said unto him, go, return on thy way to the wilderness of Damascus, and when thou comest, anoint Haziel to be king over Syria.
point_summary: God's command to Elijah in verse 15 — return and anoint Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha — demonstrates that even a prophet who begged to die still had an unfinished, God-appointed task awaiting him.

context: Jehu, the son of Nimshi, shall thou anoint to be king over Israel, and Elisha, the son of Shaphat of Abel Meholah, shalt thou anoint to be prophet in thy room.
point_summary: God's command in verse 16 to anoint Jehu as king and Elisha as prophet demonstrates that even a despairing, death-wishing Elijah still had a specific, unfinished task assigned to him by God.

context: And it shall come to pass that him that escapeth the sword of Haziel shall Jehu slay, and him that escapeth from the sword of Jehu shall Elisha slay.
point_summary: God's threefold commission to Elijah in verses 15–17—anointing Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha—demonstrates that divine purpose persists even when a servant is ready to quit.

context: We think about this recurring question in 1 Kings 19. What doest thou hear, Elijah?
point_summary: Jezebel's threat drove Elijah from his post, illustrating how enemy intimidation aims to displace believers from the God-assigned task they are called to fulfill.

context: with regard to the subject that we're dealing with from 1 Kings 19, when God says to Elijah, what doest thou hear, Elijah?
point_summary: God's question to Elijah — "What doest thou here?" — is applied to the congregation through Mordecai's perspective, pressing believers to recognize that God has placed them in this present difficult age for such a time as this.

context: I know that God words it, what doest thou hear, Elijah? But if I could say it this evening and I could ask us tonight by closing and beginning our invitation, what are we doing here? I have asked myself that question so many times this week as God just arrested my attention on 1 Kings 19.
point_summary: God's question to Elijah — "What doest thou here?" — is applied as a present-tense summons, pressing believers to examine whether their lives, individually and as families, are fulfilling their singular purpose of bringing glory to God.

context: You remember Elijah, he kind of had that that it's only me syndrome, right? And we can read of that, we won't take the time to go back to 1st Kings 19 and read all that had transpired there, but he has that idea, I am the only one.
point_summary: God's reminder to the isolated Elijah that seven thousand had not bowed to Baal illustrates that believers are never truly alone, countering the "it's only me" syndrome.

context: It's mindful of what we find back in 1 Kings chapter 17 with the widow woman at Zarephath, and you think about the situation with the meal and Elisha, and she says, look, we're going to go eat this meal, and we're going to die. We've got nothing left, and Elijah said, that's fine, but make for me first, right? I mean, read 1 Kings 17.
point_summary: Like the widow of Zarephath who knew the size of what she had yet obeyed Elijah's seemingly impossible instruction, this woman understood her limited supply but trusted God's provision anyway.

context: You remember the situation with Elijah at Mount Carmel? He challenged all of those Baal worshippers, and he challenged them. And we find the Bible tells us if you were to read the passage of Scripture there in 1 Kings, in chapter number 18, you begin to understand all that's happening in the challenge, and you find that Elijah says to them that
point_summary: Elijah's contest on Mount Carmel illustrates by contrast that the God of Israel, unlike Baal, genuinely hears and answers prayer — a reality that itself warrants praise even before the answer arrives.

context: And here's what he says, and ye call, verse number 24, call ye on the name of your gods, and I will call on the name of the Lord. The God that answereth by fire, let him be God.
point_summary: Elijah's challenge at Carmel—"call on the name of your gods, and I will call on the name of the LORD"—is used to contrast the deaf silence of false gods with the assurance that the living God genuinely hears and answers prayer.

context: Elijah would pray and God answers him, but it's amazing as you look at the prayer that he prays, it's recorded for us in verse 36
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Mount Carmel (1 Kings 18:36–37) is cited as evidence that, unlike Baal who could not hear, Israel's God genuinely hears and answers when his people call upon him.

context: and verse 37 you see the response, and I can rejoice this morning that our God hears us.
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Mount Carmel (1 Kings 18:37) illustrates by contrast with the deaf Baal that Israel's God genuinely hears and answers when his people call upon him.

context: if you were to read 1 Kings chapter 17, how Elijah just, he's on the scene. And here's this man, Elijah, a prophet of the Lord that stands before the king of Israel and delivers a message of judgment. And I mean, he just appears on the pages of scripture in 1 Kings 17 and verse number one.
point_summary: Elijah's sudden, unannounced appearance in 1 Kings 17:1 is used as a parallel to illustrate how Oded likewise emerges abruptly onto the pages of Scripture as a prophet delivering a bold message.

context: you read in first Kings chapter 17 You begin to read about the life of Elijah and how he prayed
point_summary: Elijah's life of fervent prayer in 1 Kings 17–18, cited via James, illustrates that effectual, fervent prayer of a righteous man accomplishes much, grounding Samuel's own crying out to God.

context: in first Kings 17 and 18
point_summary: The contrast between Baal's silence and God's responsiveness to Samuel's prayer illustrates that only the living God hears and answers those who cry out to him.

context: in first Kings 17 and 18 and First Kings 18
point_summary: The impotence of Baal's idol—which cannot hear, see, or respond—contrasts directly with the living God who hears and answers when his people cry out in prayer.

context: you can see first Kings chapter number two in particular
point_summary: The generational chain of fatherly instruction—David to Solomon, Solomon to Rehoboam—is grounded in 1 Kings 2:1 as the historical moment David called Solomon and passed on his charge.

context: Just where I was was in first Kings dealing with how he Solomon loved many strange women and all that situation with the Idolatry
point_summary: Solomon's failure to follow his own wisdom—loving many strange women and falling into idolatry—is cited to show that reading Proverbs merely as Solomon's counsel to Rehoboam yields less than reading it as the Heavenly Father's instruction to His children.

context: You'll remember back in 1 Kings 19, if you hold your place here in 2 Kings 8 and just quickly go back to 1 Kings 19
point_summary: In the broader narrative context, 1 Kings 19 is recalled to show that God's commissioning of Elijah—despite his fear and despair after Jezebel's threat—included the anointing of Hazael as king over Syria.

context: Elijah, excuse me, had experienced in chapter number 18, great victory over the worshipers of Baal
point_summary: Elijah's great victory over Baal's prophets in 1 Kings 18 is recalled as the immediate backdrop to his collapse in chapter 19, showing that spiritual triumph can precede overwhelming fear.

context: But it was in chapter number 19
point_summary: God's sovereign commissioning of Elijah in 1 Kings 19—after his overwhelming fear of Jezebel—is recalled to show that divine appointment of Hazael as king of Syria traces back to that earlier mandate given to Elijah.

context: the Lord said unto him, Go, return on thy way to the wilderness of Damascus. And when thou comest, anoint Hazael to be king over Syria.
point_summary: God's earlier command to Elijah in 1 Kings 19:15 to anoint Hazael king over Syria finds its fulfillment now in 2 Kings 8, confirming the long-range reliability of divine prophecy.

context: but King Solomon loved many strange women together with the daughter of Pharaoh, women of the Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, Zidaneans and Hittites.
point_summary: Solomon's marriage to Edomite women, listed among his many foreign wives in 1 Kings 11:1, is cited as one link in the ongoing thread of Edomite interaction with Israel across successive kings.

context: And the Bible then tells us in verse 14 of 1 Kings 11, and the Lord stirred up an adversary unto Solomon, hey, dad, the Edomite.
point_summary: God's judgment on Solomon for his idolatry, provoked by his Edomite and other foreign wives, resulted in the Lord raising up Hadad the Edomite as an adversary against him.

context: You think about Elijah. Elijah, we think so often of calling fire down from heaven. I mean, he prayed. It was a great scene as you study there in 1 Kings.
point_summary: Elijah's collapse into despair shortly after his greatest triumph in 1 Kings illustrates that even the spiritually strong can become weak and need others to come alongside them.

context: 17, 1 Kings chapter number 17.
point_summary: God's care for his children, illustrated through the events of 1 Kings 17, is presented as evidence of his surpassing greatness — a greatness that exceeds all human comprehension yet remains personally knowable.

context: One of the aspects as we began looking a couple of weeks ago at this portion of scripture in 1 Kings is dealing with the fact that God's care for his children reveals to us how great he is.
point_summary: God's care for his children, illustrated in the Elijah narrative of 1 Kings 17, is presented as a window into his greatness—because a God who cares is a great God.

context: We find that as we began studying in 1 Kings chapter 17, we find that God's care was given, was evidence to us as we look at the interactions with Elijah as God would give him direction to go to the brook Cherith.
point_summary: God's unlimited, personal care for every believer is illustrated in 1 Kings 17 through His specific provision for Elijah at Cherith—directing ravens to bring food and the brook to supply water—demonstrating that divine care is both global and intimate.

context: the ravens brought him bread and flesh in the morning and bread and flesh in the evening and he drank of the brook
point_summary: God's miraculous provision of bread and flesh through ravens twice daily at Cherith demonstrates His intimate, personal care for His people, even overriding the natural instincts of scavenger birds.

context: the Bible tells us there in verse number seven that the brook dried up
point_summary: The drying of the brook at Cherith was no surprise to God, who had himself decreed the drought, and its failure became the occasion for fresh divine direction rather than divine neglect.

context: and the word of the Lord came unto him saying, arise, get thee to Zarephath, which belongeth to Zidon, and dwell there.
point_summary: God's renewed word to Elijah after the brook dried up demonstrates that divine provision does not end when one source fails, because God always has a plan for his people individually.

context: Behold, I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee.
point_summary: God's command to Elijah—"Get thee to Zarephath"—is presented as evidence that God always has a plan, both for the ages and for his people individually.

context: The Bible says this, and this is the. there with Obadiah, but it says in verse number four, for it was so when Jezebel cut off the prophets of the Lord that Obadiah took in a hundred prophets and hid them by fifty in a cave and fed them with bread and water.
point_summary: Jezebel's slaughter of the Lord's prophets and Obadiah's covert rescue of a hundred of them establish that Elijah was a marked man living under active, state-sponsored persecution.

context: We see also in verse number 13 that it says this, was it not told my Lord what I did when Jezebel slew the prophets of the Lord, how I hid in a hundred men of the Lord's prophets by fifty in a cave and fed them with bread and water?
point_summary: Obadiah's rehearsal of hiding a hundred prophets from Jezebel establishes that Elijah operated as a marked man in a kingdom actively hunting and killing the Lord's servants.

context: As the Lord thy God liveth, there is no nation or kingdom whither my Lord hath not sent to seek, who? Thee.
point_summary: Ahab's exhaustive, oath-backed international manhunt for Elijah—searching every nation and kingdom—establishes that Elijah traveled to Zarephath as a fugitive whose life was in constant danger.

context: fetch me, I pray thee, a morsel of bread in thine hand
point_summary: Elijah's request for bread in verse 11 reveals the full extent of what God was asking the widow to provide — not just water, but her last remaining food.

context: as the Lord thy God liveth, I have not a cake, but a handful of meal in a barrel, and a little oil in a cruise
point_summary: The widow's confession in verse 12 reveals the desperate scarcity of her condition — only a handful of meal and a little oil remaining — making her the unlikely and seemingly impossible vessel God had appointed to sustain Elijah.

context: he says to her in verse number 13, fear not, but notice what he says, go and do as thou hast said, but make me thereof a little cake first, and bring it unto me, and after make for thee and for thy son.
point_summary: Elijah's seemingly self-serving command—"make me a cake first"—was not selfishness but confident trust in God's specific promise that the widow would sustain him.

context: for thus saith the Lord God of Israel, verse number 14, the barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruise of oil fail until the day that the Lord sendeth rain upon the earth.
point_summary: God's promise through Elijah that the meal and oil would not fail until rain came grounded Elijah's bold request in divine provision, not selfishness — he was trusting a specific word from God.

context: Notice what it says in verse 16. Can you imagine? It says in the barrel of meal, wasted not. I got that underlined in my Bible. Neither did the cruise of oil fail.
point_summary: God's sustaining power is displayed in the miraculous, ongoing provision of meal and oil across many days, fulfilling precisely what He had spoken through Elijah.

context: His care is evidenced in 1 Kings 17 through providing direction to Elijah
point_summary: God's unlimited care for his people is evidenced in 1 Kings 17, where he provides Elijah specific direction and provision even after the brook dries up, demonstrating nothing is too hard for him.

context: And the Bible tells us that the meal wasted not, neither did the cruise of oil fail according to the word of the Lord, which he spake by Elijah.
point_summary: God's supernatural provision of unending meal and oil for Elijah, the widow, and her son demonstrates that the unlimited God faithfully fulfills his word to care for his people.

context: One of the other things I learned about Ethan was in 2 Chronicles, I'm sorry, 1 Kings 4, verse 31, that his wisdom is compared to the wisdom of Solomon.
point_summary: Ethan the Ezraite's mention alongside Solomon in 1 Kings 4:31 establishes his exceptional wisdom and lends weight to his authorship of Psalm 89 as a credible, Spirit-gifted instructor of God's people.

context: There's not going to be a 1 Kings 17 and 18 where you see the miracles
point_summary: The miracles of Elijah in 1 Kings 17–18 are held up as evidence that biblical faith must move from proclamation to practice, or there is no account to record.

context: There's not going to be a 1 Kings 17 and 18 where you see the miracles and then you see the situation in 1 Kings 18 with the showdown at Carmel
point_summary: The Mount Carmel confrontation in 1 Kings 18 is cited as evidence that Elijah's miraculous stand required him to act on what he proclaimed, illustrating that faith must move from confession to practice.

context: I think about Elijah in 1 Kings chapter 18 and verse number 21. We're familiar with the text of scripture when Elijah has that opportunity at Mount Carmel to have that fire come from heaven, but before the fire ever comes from heaven and consumes the offering, he stands before the people that are gathered together and he says, how long halt ye between two opinions? If God be God, follow him, but if Baal, then follow him.
point_summary: Refusing to answer Elijah's challenge between God and Baal is itself a choice — silence and delay constitute a decision against responding to God.

context: You go back to 1 Kings 17 and 18, and you read the prayer of Elijah
point_summary: Elijah's prayer in 1 Kings 17–18, cited by James as the model of effectual, fervent prayer, stands as part of the Old Testament's broader record of answered prayer that informed Paul's own commitment to prayer.

context: You go back to 1 Kings 17 and 18, and you read the prayer of Elijah
point_summary: Elijah's prayer in 1 Kings 17–18, cited by James as the model of effectual, fervent prayer, stands as one of the Old Testament's recorded examples of answered prayer that informed Paul's own commitment to prayer.

context: in verse number 1 of 1st Kings 17, we are introduced to Elijah
point_summary: Elijah's sudden 25-word declaration of drought in 1 Kings 17:1 illustrates that famine is an instrument of divine judgment against national wickedness, confirming God's sovereign control over rain and harvest.

context: If you were to take the time to study the latter part of 1st Kings chapter 16, you'll find that the king is Ahab
point_summary: The wickedness of Ahab's reign in 1 Kings 16 provides the backdrop for understanding the drought declared in 1 Kings 17:1 as an act of divine judgment against Israel's sin.

context: here's Elijah in 1st Kings 17, in verse number 1
point_summary: Elijah's 25-word declaration in 1 Kings 17:1 is presented as a paradigm case of famine as divine judgment, directly tied to Ahab's unprecedented wickedness in Israel.

context: In chapter 18, look at verse number 1
point_summary: Famine in 1 Kings 18:1 is presented as the direct consequence of divine judgment, fulfilling Elijah's declaration that rain would cease because of Israel's wickedness under Ahab.

context: Ahab says in verse 17, Art thou he that troubleth
point_summary: Famine in Israel under Ahab is presented as direct divine judgment upon national wickedness, with Elijah's prophetic word withholding rain serving as the instrument of that judgment.

context: As the Lord God of Israel liveth before whom I stand, there shall not be dew nor rain these years, but according to my word
point_summary: Elijah's 25-word declaration in 1 Kings 17:1 is presented as a direct instance of famine functioning as divine judgment against Ahab's unprecedented wickedness in Israel.

context: Elijah says this in verse 18, I have not troubled Israel, but thou and thy father's house, and that ye have forsaken the commandments of the Lord, and thou hast followed Balaam
point_summary: Elijah's declaration in 1 Kings 18:18 establishes that famine is not the prophet's doing but the direct consequence of Israel's forsaking God's commandments under Ahab's leadership.

context: Turn over to 1 Kings chapter 3, if you will. 1 Kings chapter 3.
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom in 1 Kings 3 is presented as a living illustration of the believer's need to seek God's wisdom rather than relying on human understanding when facing great responsibility.

context: In 1 Kings chapter 3, beginning in verse number 5, the Bible says in Gibeon, the Lord appeared to Solomon in a dream by night, and God said, ask what I shall give thee.
point_summary: Solomon's experience of God inviting him to ask for anything illustrates the sermon's call to seek divine wisdom directly from God, as James 1:5 instructs.

context: God says, ask what I shall give thee
point_summary: Solomon's response to God's open-ended offer — acknowledging he lacked wisdom to lead — illustrates the posture James 1:5 calls believers to: recognizing personal insufficiency and asking God directly for wisdom.

context: Thou hast showed unto thy servant David my father great mercy, according as he walked before thee in truth and in righteousness and in uprightness of heart with thee
point_summary: Solomon's response to God's open-ended offer — acknowledging "I am but a little child, I know not how to go out or come in" — illustrates the posture of recognizing one's own lack of wisdom before asking God to supply it.

context: God said, ask what I shall give thee
point_summary: God's open invitation to Solomon — "Ask what I shall give thee" — illustrates that when believers acknowledge their lack of wisdom and ask God for it, such a request pleases Him.

context: I'm but a little child, I know not how to go out or come in
point_summary: Solomon's confession in 1 Kings 3:7 — "I am but a little child, I know not how to go out or come in" — is presented as a model acknowledgment of personal inadequacy and lack of wisdom before God.

context: And thy servant is in the midst of thy people, which thou hast chosen a great people that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude
point_summary: Solomon's confession in 1 Kings 3:8 — that he stood amid a great and unnumbered people he could not lead alone — illustrates the self-aware acknowledgment of inadequacy that James 1:5 calls believers to bring before God in prayer.

context: Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad, for who is able to judge this thy so great a people
point_summary: Solomon's request for an understanding heart to judge God's people illustrates the posture James 1:5 calls for: acknowledging one's lack of wisdom and asking God directly to supply it.

context: and the speech, notice what it says, pleased the Lord
point_summary: Asking God for wisdom—rather than wealth or honor—pleased the Lord, illustrating that God is not bothered by such requests but is troubled when we fail to make them.

context: if you weren't familiar with 1 Kings 3
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom above long life or riches so pleased God that it stands as the model for what James 1:5 promises—God gives wisdom generously to those who ask.

context: verse number 11, and God said unto him, because thou hast asked this thing, and then notice what he says, and hast not asked for thyself a long life
point_summary: God's pleasure in Solomon's request is highlighted by what Solomon did *not* ask for—long life, riches, or vengeance—choosing instead understanding to discern judgment.

context: verse 12, I have done according to thy words, No, notice what God says. I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart, so that there was none like thee before thee, neither after thee shall any arise like unto thee
point_summary: God's response to Solomon's request for wisdom — granting not only an unparalleled wise and understanding heart but also the riches and honor Solomon never asked for — illustrates that seeking wisdom first brings blessings beyond what was requested.

context: and I have also given thee that which thou hast not asked, both riches and honor, so that there shall not be any among you, any among the kings like unto thee all thy days
point_summary: God's pleasure with Solomon's request for wisdom rather than wealth or long life led Him to grant those very things as an unrequested bonus, illustrating that seeking wisdom first results in additional blessings.

context: if thou wilt walk in my ways to keep my statutes and my commandments as the Lord David did walk, then I will lengthen thy days
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And Solomon awoke, and behold, it was a dream. And he came to Jerusalem and stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord and offered up burnt offerings and offered peace offerings and made a feast to all his servants
point_summary: Solomon's awakening from the dream and his immediate worship before the ark illustrates that God's gift of wisdom was received and responded to in reverent gratitude before it was ever put to use.

context: Then came there two women that were harlots unto the king and stood before him
point_summary: Solomon's request for wisdom to govern God's people is immediately tested by the arrival of two harlots with an unresolvable dispute, illustrating that divine wisdom is given for the practical work of rendering judgment.

context: I'm just a child. I don't know how to come in or go out, go out or come in. He says that I need wisdom
point_summary: Solomon's request for an understanding heart to discern judgment—rather than long life, riches, or vengeance—is presented as the model of wisdom-seeking that pleased God and resulted in blessing beyond what was asked.

context: I've done according to thy words. I've given thee a wise and understanding heart.
point_summary: God's gift of wisdom to Solomon is immediately tested by an impossible human dispute, illustrating that divine wisdom is given precisely for the real, unsolvable situations life brings before us.

context: O my Lord, I and this woman dwell in one house, and I was delivered of a child with her in the house.
point_summary: Wisdom given by God is immediately tested by an impossible human dispute, illustrating why Solomon needed divine understanding rather than natural discernment to judge rightly.

context: all four of them would be in that house. No strangers were there.
point_summary: Verse 18 establishes the closed, witness-free setting of the two women's dispute, framing the impossible judgment Solomon faces as the immediate test of the God-given wisdom he had just received.

context: this woman's child died in the night because she overlaid it. And she arose at midnight and took my son from beside me while thine handmaid slept and laid it in her bosom and laid her dead child in my bosom.
point_summary: Wisdom's necessity is illustrated by the impossible dispute Solomon faced—a dead child, a living child, two conflicting claims, and no human means of discernment apart from God-given wisdom.

context: and the king said, verse number 24, Bring me a sword. They brought a sword before the king. And the king said, Divide the living child in two and give half to the one and half to the other.
point_summary: Solomon's command to divide the living child—wisdom no human mind would naturally devise—illustrates that God-given wisdom produces discernment beyond ordinary human reasoning.

context: then spake the woman whose the living child was unto the king, for her bowels yearned upon her son, and she said, O my Lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it. But the other said, Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it.
point_summary: Solomon's God-given wisdom—asked for before the crisis arose—exposed the true mother through her instinctive, self-sacrificing love, a discernment no human strategy could have produced.

context: Then the king answered, verse 27, and said, Give her the living child, and in no wise slay it. She is the mother thereof.
point_summary: Solomon's wise verdict in 1 Kings 3:27 is presented as a direct demonstration that God-given wisdom, received simply by asking, was visibly at work in him.

context: Verse 28, notice what the Bible says in verse 28, in all Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged, and they feared the king, for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to do judgment.
point_summary: Solomon's God-given wisdom, publicly recognized as divine rather than human in origin, illustrates that wisdom received through asking God brings observable, judgment-executing results that glorify God, not the recipient.

context: as you consider the truth of this particular event in 1 Kings 3, we see the principle that we're studying
point_summary: Solomon's God-given wisdom in resolving the disputed child (1 Kings 3:26–28) illustrates James 1's principle that divine wisdom, visibly distinct from human reasoning, is obtained simply by asking God for it.

context: Look in chapter 4 of 1 Kings, look in chapter 4, look at verse 29. The Bible says this, "...and God gave Solomon wisdom and understanding exceeding much, and largeness of heart, even as the sand that is on the seashore."
point_summary: God's gift of wisdom to Solomon—described as exceeding much and vast as the sand of the seashore—illustrates the James 1 principle that wisdom comes by asking God, not by human effort or understanding.

context: 1 Kings 4 and verse 29, the Bible says that God gave Solomon wisdom
point_summary: God's gift of wisdom to Solomon in 1 Kings 4:29 illustrates that divine wisdom surpasses all human understanding, standing in direct contrast to the counsel the world offers on finances, marriage, and family.

context: verse number 31, there in chapter four, it says this, for he was wiser than all men
point_summary: Solomon's surpassing wisdom over all men is cited as direct evidence that God-given wisdom is categorically different from the world's counsel on finances, marriage, and family.

context: Sometimes if we're not careful, we can get like Elijah. I'm the only one. Amen? Sometimes you can get like Elijah. I'm the only one. No one else.
point_summary: Elijah's complaint "I am the only one" illustrates the isolating discouragement believers can feel, which Paul counters by assuring the Corinthians that other saints stand with them.

context: verse number 12 with you this morning
point_summary: Widow of Zarephath's confession of having only a handful of meal and a little oil illustrates how material lack can bring God's people to the edge of despair.

context: look at verse 11 with it
point_summary: Elijah's request to the widow of Zarephath in a time of famine illustrates how material lack can produce discouragement, even to the point of despair, among God's people.

context: he called to her and said, bring me, I pray, the morsel of bread in thine hand
point_summary: The widow of Zarephath's declaration that she would cook her last meal and die illustrates how material lack can produce profound discouragement even among those within the sphere of God's providential care.

context: as the Lord thy God liveth, I have not a cake, but a handful of meal in a barrel and a little oil and cruise
point_summary: The widow of Zarephath's declaration that she had only a handful of meal and planned to eat it and die illustrates how material lack can bring deep discouragement even among those within God's providential care.

context: Behold, I am gathering two sticks that I may go in and dress it for me and my son
point_summary: The widow of Zarephath's declaration that she had only a handful of meal and planned to eat it and die illustrates how material lack can produce profound discouragement even among those within God's providential care.

context: that we may eat it and die
point_summary: The widow of Zarephath's declaration that she had only a handful of meal and planned to eat it and die illustrates how material lack can produce deep discouragement even among those within God's providential care.

context: As you were to continue to read in 1 Kings chapter 17, God provided for her and her son the blessing that God gave.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Jehoshaphat comes along and he made affinity with him
point_summary: When God's word confronts a heart that is not right, the response will be either repentance or rage — as Asa's wrathful imprisonment of the prophet Hanani illustrates.

context: Elijah is mocking the followers of Baal with that type of terminology because of the fact that Baal wasn't answering them and they were pleading and pleading and he says he's on a journey maybe He's asleep
point_summary: Elijah's mockery of Baal as sleeping or on a journey contrasts sharply with the true God, who rested on the seventh day not from exhaustion but to establish a pattern for Israel.

context: You remember the widow in Zarephath? Elijah? You remember? I only have enough to make enough for me and my son and we're going to die
point_summary: In deep poverty, the widow of Zarephath—like the Macedonian churches—illustrates that God can still use what little remains when a person surrenders everything they have.

context: the meal wasted not. Her and her son and Elijah, they were sustained for many days because she believed the prophet
point_summary: Obedience to the prophet's counterintuitive command—giving her last meal first—resulted in supernatural, sustained provision, illustrating that faithful giving from scarcity defies human reasoning.

context: he had the ravens come and feed him
point_summary: God's provision for Elijah through ravens is cited as evidence that God can provide miraculously in any manner he chooses, underscoring that the manna's specific process was a deliberate test of obedience, not a necessity.

context: As we examine the passage of Scripture this morning in 1 Kings chapter 2
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 frames a father's final opportunity to influence and instruct his son, a moment that will come for every person without exception.

context: Notice in verse number one, the Bible says, Now the days of David drew nigh that he should die.
point_summary: The universal certainty of death, illustrated by David's approaching end, presses every listener to ask whether they are prepared for that appointed moment.

context: Now, the days of David drew nigh that he should die.
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon frames the universal reality of death as the solemn context from which a father's final command to live faithfully before God gains its urgency.

context: And he charged Solomon, his son, saying, I go the way of all the earth. Be thou strong therefore.
point_summary: Facing the certainty of death, David's charge to Solomon to "be thou strong and shew thyself a man" frames biblical manhood as inseparable from keeping God's commandments and walking in His ways.

context: And show thyself a man and keep the charge of the Lord thy God to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes, and his commandments, and his judgments, and his testimonies, as it is written in the law of Moses, that thou mayest prosper in all that thou doest.
point_summary: Obedience to God's statutes, commandments, and testimonies—as written in the law of Moses—is the condition David sets before Solomon for prospering in all he does.

context: If thy children take heed to their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart, with all their soul, there shall not fail thee, said he, a man on the throne of Israel
point_summary: God's conditional promise to sustain David's dynasty hinges on his children walking before Him in truth with all their heart and soul, linking obedience to covenant continuity on Israel's throne.

context: now the days of David drew nigh that he should die, and he charged Solomon his son
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2:1 presents a private, intimate moment that calls every believer to speak urgent, life-shaping words to loved ones now, while the opportunity remains.

context: I go the way of all the earth, and then he tells him this, be thou strong therefore and show thyself a man
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon — "be thou strong and shew thyself a man" — defines true manhood not by physical strength or cultural standards, but by a God-given standard that stands in contrast to what society prescribes.

context: be thou strong
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon to "be strong" points not to physical strength, which fades with age, but to spiritual strength that comes from God alone working through the believer.

context: show thyself a man
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon to "be strong" points not to physical strength, which fades with age, but to spiritual strength that comes from God alone working through the believer.

context: God allowed him in 1 Kings chapter number 2 to have this desire fulfilled in being able to charge his son Solomon
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 is presented as the fulfillment of the psalmist's desire to declare God's strength to the next generation, beginning with one's own children.

context: this moment between father and son in 1 Kings chapter 2
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 is presented as the fulfillment of the psalmist's desire to declare God's strength to the next generation, making the father-son moment spiritually significant for both men.

context: Be strong, show thyself a man, walk with God
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2:2 is shown here to have borne fruit across generations, as Solomon's own words in Proverbs 4 echo what his father taught him and then pass that same instruction to Rehoboam.

context: we read this passage in 1 Kings chapter 4
point_summary: The charge David gave Solomon was passed to Rehoboam in the next generation, illustrating the perpetuity of a godly father's instruction across successive generations.

context: the characteristics that we find in 1 Kings chapter number two are characteristics of a godly individual
point_summary: David's deathbed charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 is presented as the biblical pattern of a father teaching godly characteristics to the next generation, consistent with Deuteronomy 6, Psalm 78, and Ephesians 6.

context: and keep the charge of the Lord thy God, notice, to walk in His ways
point_summary: Obedience to God's statutes, commandments, judgments, and testimonies is framed not as a means of earning or keeping salvation, but as the Spirit-enabled walk that pleases God and exalts Christ.

context: we find here in the text of Scripture in 1 Kings chapter 2 and verses 3 and 4
point_summary: Walking in the ways of the Lord—keeping His statutes, commandments, judgments, and testimonies—is presented not as a means of earning or keeping salvation, but as the life that pleases God after regeneration.

context: David says to Solomon, be strong, show thyself a man and he gives him this charge to walk in the ways of the Lord, to keep his statutes, his commandments, his judgments, his testimonies
point_summary: Godliness is not accidental but requires purposeful submission and surrender, as David's charge to Solomon to keep God's statutes, commandments, judgments, and testimonies demonstrates.

context: as it is written in the law of Moses back in 1 Kings chapter two
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 frames obedience to God's statutes as the condition for divine blessing and favor—a prosperity defined by God's terms, not man's.

context: that thou mayest prosper in all that thou doest and withersoever thou turnest thyself
point_summary: David's charge to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 frames obedience to God's statutes as the condition for divine blessing and favor, a prosperity defined by God's terms rather than man's.

context: I hope everyone here this morning would take the truth of 1 Kings chapter 2 and we analyze this moment between a father and a son
point_summary: The deathbed charge of David to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 is presented as a timeless call to examine one's own obedience to God's commandments, statutes, judgments, and testimonies as the pathway to divine blessing and favor.

context: The significance of the moment in 1 Kings chapter 2 must not pass us by
point_summary: The deathbed charge of David to Solomon in 1 Kings 2 is presented as a timeless call to examine personal obedience to God's commandments, statutes, judgments, and testimonies as the pathway to divine favor and blessing.

context: the widow of Zarephath. We're gonna eat this little bit, and we're gonna die. We got nothing left.
point_summary: Childlike faith that trusts God without condition is illustrated by the widow of Zarephath's obedience—giving her last meal first—and God's faithfulness in return: the meal wasted not.

context: the meal wasted not
point_summary: God's faithfulness to the widow of Zarephath—her meal not wasting despite certain scarcity—is cited as evidence that God never drops those who obey and trust Him.

context: You can read about his fame and all that he brought in
point_summary: Solomon's importation of exotic animals into Israel illustrates how rulers historically transplanted creatures across climates, producing the adaptations that some mistakenly cite as evidence for evolution.

context: And it came to pass at the time of the offering and the evening sacrifice that Elijah the prophet came near
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Mount Carmel illustrates how intimate knowledge of God's name and character produces the courage and confidence to call on Him publicly before enemies.

context: Lord God of Abraham, Isaac, and of Israel, let it be known this day that thou art God in Israel and that I am thy servant and that I have done all these things at thy word
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Carmel, invoking the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel by name, demonstrates that confident, courageous prayer flows from knowing who God is.

context: Hear me, O Lord, hear me, that this people may know that thou art the Lord God and that thou hast turned their heart back again
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Mount Carmel demonstrates that praying with courage and confidence flows directly from knowing who God is.

context: Then the fire of the Lord fell
point_summary: Elijah's prayer at Mount Carmel, addressed to the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, demonstrates that confident, courageous intercession flows from knowing who God truly is.

reference_text: God commanded the ravens to feed Elijah
context: Just as God commanded the ravens to feed Elijah
point_summary: God's sovereign command over the ravens to feed Elijah is cited as a parallel demonstrating that He, as Creator, likewise directed the animals to the ark without Noah's effort.

context: You read the book of First Kings, Second Kings
point_summary: The repeated pattern in 1 Kings and 2 Kings of each generation abandoning the faith as merely their parents' God warns that authentic, visible walk with God must be passed to the next generation.
